questions.question: A $2\text{ kg}$ ball is dropped from a height of $10\text{ m}$. Using $g = 9.8\text{ m/s}^2$, what is its kinetic energy just before it hits the ground? (Assume no air resistance.)
questions.explanation: PE at top $= mgh = 2 \times 9.8 \times 10 = 196\text{ J}$. At ground level, all PE is converted to KE, so KE $= 196\text{ J}$.

questions.question: A $2\text{ kg}$ ball is dropped from $10\text{ m}$. What is its potential energy when it has fallen halfway ($5\text{ m}$ above the ground)? $(g = 9.8\text{ m/s}^2)$
questions.explanation: $PE = mgh = 2 \times 9.8 \times 5 = 98\text{ J}$. At the midpoint, half the original PE remains.

questions.question: A roller coaster car with total mechanical energy of $5,000\text{ J}$ at the top of a hill arrives at the bottom with $3,800\text{ J}$ of kinetic energy. How much energy was lost to friction?
questions.explanation: Energy lost $= 5,000 - 3,800 = 1,200\text{ J}$. This energy was converted to heat and sound by friction.

questions.question: A $3\text{ kg}$ rock falls from rest at a height of $20\text{ m}$. Using energy conservation, what is its speed just before hitting the ground? $(g = 9.8\text{ m/s}^2$, ignore air resistance.)
A light bulb receives $100\text{ J}$ of electrical energy. It produces $15\text{ J}$ of light and the rest is heat. According to the law of conservation of energy, how much energy becomes heat?
questions.explanation: Energy cannot be destroyed. Total input $= 100\text{ J}$. Useful light $= 15\text{ J}$. Heat $= 100 - 15 = 85\text{ J}$.

questions.question: A student states: "Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only changed from one form to another." Which law is this?
questions.explanation: The Law of Conservation of Energy states that total energy in a closed system remains constant — it can only be transformed.

questions.question: A pendulum bob of mass $0.5\text{ kg}$ swings from a height of $0.8\text{ m}$ to its lowest point (height $= 0\text{ m}$). How much kinetic energy does it have at the lowest point? $(g = 9.8\text{ m/s}^2)$
questions.explanation: $\Delta PE = mg\Delta h = 0.5 \times 9.8 \times 0.8 = 3.92\text{ J}$. At the lowest point, all PE is converted to KE, so KE $= 3.92\text{ J}$.

questions.question: Using the pendulum from the previous question, what is the speed of the bob at the lowest point?
A bouncing ball loses height with each bounce. According to the law of conservation of energy, what happens to the "lost" mechanical energy?
options: It is destroyed by the impact with the floor
It is converted to heat and sound energy
It is stored as gravitational potential energy
It remains as mechanical energy in the ball
questions.explanation: Total energy is conserved. The mechanical energy not recovered is converted into heat and sound during the bounce.

questions.question: A $0.2\text{ kg}$ ball is dropped from $2\text{ m}$ and bounces back to $1.5\text{ m}$. How much energy was lost during the bounce? $(g = 9.8\text{ m/s}^2)$
questions.explanation: PE before $= 0.2 \times 9.8 \times 2 = 3.92\text{ J}$. PE after $= 0.2 \times 9.8 \times 1.5 = 2.94\text{ J}$. Energy lost $= 3.92 - 2.94 = 0.98\text{ J}$.

questions.question: A sled slides down a hill. The total mechanical energy at the top is $2,400\text{ J}$. At the bottom, the sled has $1,800\text{ J}$ of kinetic energy. How much work was done by friction?
questions.explanation: Energy lost $= 2,400 - 1,800 = 600\text{ J}$. Friction does negative work because it opposes motion, so work done by friction $= -600\text{ J}$.

questions.question: As an object falls freely (no air resistance), which of the following is true?
options: PE increases, KE decreases, total energy is constant
PE decreases, KE decreases, total energy decreases
PE decreases, KE increases, total energy is constant
PE decreases, KE increases, total energy increases
questions.explanation: As it falls, PE converts to KE. Total mechanical energy (PE + KE) remains constant when no energy is lost to friction or air resistance.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You've mastered the Law of Conservation of Energy and energy calculations!
scoreMessages.70: Good work! Review calculating energy loss in systems with friction.
scoreMessages.50: Keep practicing! Focus on PE = mgh, KE = ½mv², and the principle that total energy is conserved.
scoreMessages.0: Review the basics: energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed. PE + KE + losses = constant.
